text:
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Surveillance Visit — Post-Treatment, 6 Months Off Systemic Therapy

ATTENDING: Hematology/Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, outpatient follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 58-year-old woman with a history of Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma, now presenting for her 6-month post-treatment surveillance visit following planned completion of nivo after 2 years of total checkpoint inhibitor therapy. She comes in today for routine labs, symptom review, and discussion of recent surveillance imaging results.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo was originally diagnosed approximately 3 years ago with Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, M1c) cutaneous malignant melanoma arising from the left posterior shoulder, presenting with a 4.2 mm Breslow thickness, Clark level V primary lesion with ulceration. At initial staging, she had bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, multiple subcutaneous nodules, and four hepatic metastases with the largest measuring 3.1 cm. LDH was elevated at 387 U/L at diagnosis. Tumor genomic profiling revealed a BRAF V600E mutation, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, PTEN R130Q mutation, and high tumor mutational burden (42 mutations/Mb). PD-L1 TPS was 45%.

She was initially treated with Tafinlar 150 mg BID plus Mekinist 2 mg daily, achieving a deep partial response — approximately 68% tumor burden reduction at best response — but experienced progression at approximately 8 months, with enlarging axillary adenopathy, growing hepatic lesions, and new small pulmonary nodules. LDH rose to 512 U/L at that time. She was transitioned to second-line ipi 1 mg/kg plus nivo 3 mg/kg (CheckMate-067 dosing) every 3 weeks.

During cycle 3 of the ipi/nivo combination, she developed grade 3 immune-mediated colitis requiring hospitalization, high-dose IV methylprednisolone, and permanent discontinuation of Yervoy. Notably, her tumors responded to the immunotherapy combination — post-hospitalization restaging imaging demonstrated a partial response. After completing the steroid taper and confirmatory flexible sigmoidoscopy showing colitis resolution, she was rechallenged on nivo 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance. She also developed immune-related hypothyroidism during nivo maintenance, managed with dose titration of levothyroxine.

Over approximately 18 months of nivo maintenance, she achieved a deepening and ultimately near-complete radiographic response. At 12 months of nivo maintenance (15 months from the start of combination immunotherapy), residual imaging showed only a single subcentimeter hepatic lesion (0.4 cm right lobe), and image-guided biopsy of that lesion demonstrated no viable melanoma — only fibrotic/necrotic tissue with pigment-laden macrophages, consistent with complete pathologic response at that site.

Opdivo was electively discontinued after a cumulative 2 years of checkpoint inhibitor therapy (approximately 18 months of Opdivo maintenance following 4 doses of combination ipi/nivo), in keeping with long-term data from CheckMate-067 and emerging evidence supporting planned treatment holidays after deep, durable responses in melanoma.

She is now 6 months into active surveillance off all systemic anti-cancer therapy. Surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain were obtained approximately one week ago. She presents today for discussion of those results and general follow-up.

Today, she reports feeling very well. She has no new complaints. Specifically, she denies:
- Dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis
- Abdominal pain, right upper quadrant discomfort, nausea, or jaundice
- New skin lesions, subcutaneous lumps, or nodules
- Diarrhea or any change in bowel habits (1-2 formed stools daily, no blood or mucus)
- Neurological symptoms including headache, visual changes, focal weakness, or seizure
- Fatigue beyond her baseline
- Night sweats or unintentional weight loss
- Myalgia, arthralgia, or joint swelling suggestive of immune-related musculoskeletal toxicity

She is tolerating levothyroxine 125 mcg daily without difficulty. She reports stable energy levels and excellent quality of life. She has returned to full-time work as a landscape architect. She is engaged in a regular exercise program (walking 4-5 days per week) and has resumed outdoor activities with appropriate sun protection measures.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: No fever, chills, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. Appetite intact. Stable weight.
Skin/Integument: No new moles, lesions, or subcutaneous nodules detected by self-examination. Wearing SPF 50+ sunscreen daily per dermatology guidance.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No headaches, vision changes, or facial swelling.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: No cough, dyspnea, or wheezing.
Gastrointestinal: Bowel function normal; 1-2 formed BMs per day, no urgency, tenesmus, blood, or mucus. No abdominal pain.
Genitourinary: No hematuria or dysuria.
Musculoskeletal: No arthralgias, myalgias, or new joint swelling.
Neurologic: No headaches, focal deficits, cognitive changes, or balance difficulties.
Endocrine: Hypothyroidism well-controlled on current levothyroxine dose. No symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism (no cold intolerance, fatigue, palpitations, or heat intolerance).
Hematologic/Lymphatic: No new lymph node swelling noticed by patient. No easy bruising or bleeding.
Psychiatric: Mood excellent. Patient verbalizes gratitude and relief. No signs of depression or anxiety brought up today; she has been seeing a counselor through the survivorship program.

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
1. Levothyroxine 125 mcg orally once daily (immune-related hypothyroidism, longstanding)
2. Omeprazole 20 mg orally once daily (GI prophylaxis, continued from prior steroid course — discussed tapering off at this visit)
3. Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) 2000 IU orally once daily
4. Calcium carbonate 500 mg orally twice daily (initiated during steroid taper, plan to reassess continued need)
5. Sunscreen SPF 50+ (applied daily per dermatology recommendation)

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (summarized for context):
1. Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma — as above, currently no evidence of disease
2. Hypothyroidism (pre-existing, on levothyroxine at lower dose prior to cancer diagnosis; exacerbated by checkpoint inhibitor-related thyroiditis)
3. Prior grade 3 immune-mediated colitis (on combination ipi/nivo; resolved; ipi permanently discontinued)
4. Remote 10 pack-year smoking history, quit >20 years ago

SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Wide local excision, left posterior shoulder (primary melanoma resection)
- Core needle biopsy, right axillary lymph node (diagnostic)
- Image-guided core needle biopsy, right hepatic lobe (residual lesion, confirmed no viable tumor)

FAMILY HISTORY: Not formally documented in this note; reviewed previously.

SOCIAL HISTORY: Landscape architect, currently working full-time. Married, two adult children. Significant outdoor sun exposure history prior to diagnosis; now adheres to sun protection measures. Former smoker (10 pack-years, quit ~20 years ago). No alcohol or recreational drug use.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 36.8 degrees C, oxygen saturation 99% on room air, weight 64.2 kg (stable from last visit), BMI 23.1.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert, oriented, in excellent spirits. Appears healthy.

Skin: Well-healed surgical scar on left posterior shoulder from wide local excision; no signs of local recurrence, satellite lesions, or in-transit disease in the surrounding skin. Bilateral forearms with faint residual hyperpigmentation from previously documented rash during dabra/tram (resolved). No new suspicious pigmented or non-pigmented lesions identified on limited skin survey; comprehensive dermatologic skin check deferred to scheduled dermatology appointment.

Head/Neck: No cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy. Thyroid: non-tender, no nodules, no goiter palpated.

Lymph Nodes: Bilateral axillary lymph nodes: no palpable adenopathy. Right axilla: soft, no discrete firmness. Left axilla: normal. Inguinal nodes: no adenopathy.

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or dullness to percussion.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema (previously had grade 2 edema on targeted therapy; fully resolved).

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No right upper quadrant tenderness. Bowel sounds present and normal in all quadrants.

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Skin of palms and soles without keratoderma or fissuring (prior grade 1 hand-foot syndrome fully resolved).

Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Gait normal and steady. No cerebellar signs.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today):
- CBC: WBC 6.1 x10^9/L (normal differential), hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, platelets 224 x10^9/L — all within normal limits
- CMP: Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, glucose 91 mg/dL, ALT 22 U/L, AST 19 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 68 U/L, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, albumin 4.3 g/dL — all within normal limits
- LDH: 155 U/L (normal; consistent with prior visit)
- TSH: 3.1 mIU/L (within normal range; well-controlled on levothyroxine 125 mcg daily)
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)
- CRP: 2.4 mg/L (normal)

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed today):
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain — obtained approximately one week prior to this visit:

Chest: No pulmonary nodules or masses identified. The previously noted right lower lobe nodules (which had shrunk to below CT resolution during Opdivo maintenance) remain absent. No pleural effusion, mediastinal adenopathy, or hilar enlargement.

Axillae/Lymph nodes: Bilateral axillary regions are unremarkable with no pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. The right axillary lymph node, which had measured up to 2.1 cm at diagnosis and subsequently normalized over the course of treatment, measures 0.4 cm and is within normal limits. Left axilla similarly normal.

Abdomen/Liver: The liver appears normal in size and enhancement without focal lesions. The previously identified residual right hepatic lesion (0.4 cm), confirmed on biopsy to represent fibrosis and treated/regressed melanoma with no viable tumor, is no longer visible on the current study. No new hepatic lesions are identified. No peritoneal implants, ascites, or retroperitoneal adenopathy.

Subcutaneous tissues: No subcutaneous nodules or masses identified. The right flank nodule that was prominent at diagnosis is absent without residual abnormality.

MRI Brain (contrast-enhanced): No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease. No parenchymal lesions, leptomeningeal enhancement, or dural metastases. This study is negative for intracranial disease — consistent with the entire treatment course during which no brain involvement was ever identified.

Overall impression: Complete radiographic response maintained 6 months after finishing all systemic therapy. No evidence of disease at any previously involved site.

---

ASSESSMENT:
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 58-year-old woman with a history of Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma with initial M1c disease (bilateral axillary nodes, subcutaneous nodules, multiple hepatic metastases) who has achieved and maintained a complete radiographic response, now 6 months following planned discontinuation of Opdivo after 2 years of checkpoint inhibitor therapy. Her most recent labs show a normal LDH, normal CBC, and normal LFTs. TSH is well-controlled. Clinically, she is entirely asymptomatic, ECOG PS 0, and appears to be in excellent health.

This is a remarkable outcome. She received two lines of systemic therapy — first-line dabra/tram (BRAF/MEK inhibition), which achieved a deep partial response lasting approximately 8 months before acquired resistance developed, followed by second-line ipi/nivo combination immunotherapy, which produced a durable, deepening response ultimately confirmed as complete on imaging and by pathology. The management course was complicated by grade 3 immune-mediated colitis requiring hospitalization, high-dose steroids, and permanent discontinuation of ipi, as well as checkpoint inhibitor-associated hypothyroidism that required levothyroxine optimization. She is now off all immunotherapy and continues only on levothyroxine as her long-term sequela from treatment.

Key active issues:
1. Metastatic melanoma — complete radiographic response maintained; no evidence of active disease
2. Immune-related hypothyroidism — stable on levothyroxine 125 mcg; thyroid function tests normal today
3. Prior grade 3 immune-mediated colitis — in clinical remission; no recurrence; bowel function normal
4. Survivorship: skin surveillance, ongoing psychological wellbeing, patient education re: recurrence signs

---

PLAN:

1. Melanoma surveillance — complete radiographic response confirmed on most recent scan. Will continue quarterly CT chest/abdomen/pelvis; next scan in approximately 3 months. MRI brain every 6 months. LDH with each visit. If surveillance imaging continues to show no evidence of disease through year 1 off therapy, plan to transition to every 6-month CT intervals thereafter, per NCCN guidelines for melanoma surveillance.

2. Skin surveillance — Patient referred to dermatology for full-body skin examination every 6 months, given elevated lifetime risk of developing a second primary melanoma (prior melanoma history, mixed Afro-Caribbean/Eastern European descent, prior significant sun exposure). Patient was counseled at length on the importance of sun protection: daily broad-spectrum SPF 50 sunscreen, protective clothing, avoidance of tanning beds, and routine self-examination of skin including scalp and extremities. Patient reports excellent adherence to sun protection already.

3. Hypothyroidism management — Current levothyroxine dose of 125 mcg daily is appropriate given today's TSH of 3.1 mIU/L. Continue current dose. Annual thyroid function monitoring (TSH, free T4) at minimum; repeat in 3 months at next oncology visit. Endocrinology follow-up as needed. Patient aware this is likely a permanent immune-related endocrinopathy and will require ongoing levothyroxine.

4. GI/Colitis follow-up — Patient remains in clinical remission from prior grade 3 immune-mediated colitis. Bowel function normal. No GI complaints. Ongoing gastroenterology follow-up per their schedule; colonoscopy surveillance as recommended by GI. Patient instructed to report any return of diarrhea, rectal bleeding, or abdominal cramping immediately. Given prior grade 3 colitis, any future immunotherapy (including re-induction with checkpoint inhibitors) would require very careful multidisciplinary discussion — noted in chart for future reference.

5. Medications — Will discontinue omeprazole at this visit given it was started for GI prophylaxis during the prednisone taper, which was completed over a year ago and there is no ongoing indication. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation: bone density scan (DEXA) ordered given prolonged corticosteroid use during colitis treatment. Will reassess calcium/vitamin D supplementation after DEXA results are available. Levothyroxine to continue.

6. Survivorship — Patient is enrolled in the cancer survivorship clinic and is meeting regularly with a survivorship counselor. She is working full-time, reports excellent quality of life, and is engaged in regular physical activity. We reviewed signs and symptoms of melanoma recurrence today, including new or changing skin lesions, new palpable lymph nodes, unexplained weight loss, abdominal pain or swelling, new neurological symptoms, or persistent cough. Patient was encouraged to call immediately if any of these develop rather than waiting for a scheduled visit.

   Discussed the current evidence base regarding surveillance intensity and the expected durability of responses following combination Yervoy/nivo in melanoma. Long-term data from CheckMate-067 and related trials support that a significant proportion of patients who achieve deep responses and complete two years of Opdivo therapy have durable disease control well beyond discontinuation. While we are cautiously optimistic, ongoing vigilance remains essential.

7. Future therapy — No systemic anti-cancer therapy is planned or indicated at this time. In the event of radiographic or clinical recurrence, management options would depend on the pattern and pace of recurrence, sites of disease, patient performance status, and time since prior therapy. Given prior grade 3 colitis, re-treatment with combination ipi/nivo would generally not be offered. Options at that time could include Opdivo monotherapy re-induction, BRAF/MEK inhibitor rechallenge depending on resistance mechanisms, or clinical trial enrollment. This is discussed in general terms only at today's visit; no specific plan needed at present given current excellent status.

8. Return to clinic — Follow-up in approximately 3 months with surveillance CT, LDH, CBC, CMP, and TSH. Patient to call with any new or concerning symptoms sooner.

Time spent with patient and family: 40 minutes, greater than 50% in counseling and care coordination.

Patient understanding verified; all questions answered. Patient expressed significant gratitude for the care team and demonstrated strong health literacy and engagement in her survivorship care plan.

ECOG Performance Status: 0
Cancer Status: No evidence of disease (complete radiographic response maintained, 6 months post-nivo completion)

Electronically signed,
Attending, Hematology/Oncology
